metric_value: Industry consensus that client-server is vision of next-generation computing; installed base technology does not yet support the vision
notes: Wrap-up finding #1; key qualification distinguishing vision from reality

metric_value: Nearly 40% of typical IT budget consumed by PCs, terminals, workstations, LANs, and client-related expenses
notes: Aberdeen research finding

metric_value: Intel 486-based and RISC-based workstations capable of most demanding C-S applications available at low price points in 1993
notes: Driving earlier-than-expected availability of client-server applications

metric_value: By 1994, powerful client platforms (commercial workstations) under $3,500 with 32-bit multitasking OS (Unix, Windows NT)
notes: Commercial workstation market range defined as $3,500-$7,000 for explosive growth

metric_value: [UNVERIFIED]
Advanced multiprocessor architecture servers will become standard platforms of mid-to-late 1990s
notes: New machines scalable from low-to-high with microprocessor board upgrades

metric_value: [UNVERIFIED]

metric_name: Novell NetWare LAN OS market position
metric_value: Dominant LAN OS market share; NetWare 4.0 moving to enterprise-wide directory services
notes: SPX/IPX and TCP/IP are dominant transport protocols for client-server

metric_value: High-end LAN market leader with sophisticated enterprise directory services
notes: More advanced than Novell at enterprise level

metric_value: Has not achieved dominance despite high product quality marks and early DEC/IBM relationships
notes: Future success depends on NT integration and stronger indirect channel distribution

metric_value: Within 3-4 years client-server systems software will evolve to object-oriented architecture
notes: NeXTSTEP, Taligent Pink, Microsoft Cairo, SunSoft D.O.E. as forerunners; no clear winner yet

metric_value: [UNVERIFIED]
100 Mbps performance; $2,000 per connection on fiber; CDDI experimenting for unshielded twisted pair
notes: ATM also being explored; network capacity will be overwhelmed by client-server applications

metric_value: Client-server computing will consume enormous amounts of network capacity; multimedia, video conferencing, high data volumes will overwhelm current capacity
notes: Key planning constraint Aberdeen highlights for IS executives

metric_value: [UNVERIFIED]
New departmental computing metaphor; message-passing and LAN-based technologies for multi-user apps
notes: Early prototype of C-S design; evolving to sophisticated multi-user applications

metric_value: Extremely powerful workstations, expanded distributed server networks, mission-critical production apps with peer-to-peer cooperative processing; single request may trigger dozens of cascading server-to-server transactions
notes: Aberdeen forward-looking characterization of late 1990s C-S

metric_value: [UNVERIFIED]

metric_name: SQL service requests as dominant C-S database pattern
metric_value: Heavy emphasis on SQL services requesting from one or more RDBMS locations on network; SPX/IPX and TCP/IP as dominant transport
notes: Current implementation pattern in 1993

metric_value: Distributed relational databases employing business policy triggers plus increasing OO programming will characterize second half of decade
notes: Clustering and two-phase commit as two major design alternatives

metric_value: Many new and innovative client types will emerge: miniaturized hand-held devices, scanners, pen-based, voice-actuated systems
notes: Hundreds of dedicated servers, each optimized for specific class of application, database, or store-and-forward task

metric_value: [UNVERIFIED]
Mainframes/minicomputers becoming hub systems: enterprise data repositories and communications processors; eventually devalued to zero
notes: Next-generation: one server = one application; prices decline to remove need for multi-app balance

metric_name: Technology gating factors for advanced C-S
metric_value: Seven key dependencies: OO software, distributed database, robust microprocessors, pre-emptive multitasking OS, network capacity, systems/network management tools, applications software availability
notes: Self-explanatory tech gating factors; all must be addressed

metric_value: Four major non-technical barriers: management vision/leadership, process reengineering skills, human resources/training, financial stability of leading suppliers
notes: Technology is only part of the problem

metric_name: Custom development as only alternative for production C-S today
metric_value: Custom development of production systems is only alternative today
notes: Wrap-up finding #3; packaged C-S production software not yet mature

metric_name: Client-server readiness for decision support today
metric_value: Rudimentary tools and components now available to begin client-server for decision support applications
notes: Wrap-up finding #2; decision support is the entry point

metric_value: Transition path: Windows -> Windows for Workgroups -> NT -> Chicago -> Cairo future OO OS
notes: Aberdeen mapping Microsoft roadmap for IS planners

metric_value: Joint PowerPC development with IBM will move Macintosh interface to AIX and RISC chip platforms
notes: Apple moving familiar Mac interface to IBM RISC/AIX
